name: Peruvian Amazon Lowlands

staple_foods.name: cacao (Criollo Amazonian)

key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 5000-8000 mg procyanidin/100g raw bean [Theobroma cacao Criollo — highest polyphenol variety; Gu et al. 2006 J Agric Food Chem 54:4057; vs 3500-5500 for Forastero]

organic_acids.acetic_acid: 2.0-4.5 g/100g [Amazonian fermented cacao — Schwan & Wheals 2004 Crit Rev Food Sci Nutr 44:205]
organic_acids.citric_acid: 1.0-2.0 g/100g [cacao — reduced from 3-4 g/100g pre-fermentation; Schwan & Wheals 2004]

staple_foods.notes: Criollo cacao is the highest-quality and rarest variety — grown in the Peruvian Amazon lowland forest-agriculture agroforestry systems. Polyphenol content (5000-8000 mg/100g) is dramatically higher than Forastero (the common commercial variety) or Trinitario. The natural experiment here is RSU-31 (Amazonian Criollo, 120m) vs RSU-25 (Oaxacan cacao in mole, ~1550m, Forastero/Trinitario) — same food (cacao) but different variety, altitude, and terroir. The altitude and shade-growing conditions in Amazonian agroforestry affect the polyphenol profile. This RSU provides the 3rd low-altitude point of the Andean altitude gradient (RSU-09 Amazon at similar altitude, RSU-10 Andes at 3500m, RSU-18 Colombia at 1600m) — connecting to cacao as the fat/flavor anchor.
data_sources: Gu et al. (2006) — Procyanidin content in Theobroma cacao varieties, J Agric Food Chem 54:4057
Schwan & Wheals (2004) — Cocoa bean fermentation, Crit Rev Food Sci Nutr 44:205

staple_foods.name: yuca (cassava)
staple_foods.notes: Cassava (yuca) is the foundational carbohydrate of Amazonian lowland food systems — capable of growing in poor acid soils at high heat where other starchy crops fail. Bitter varieties require detoxification (grating + pressing + cooking to remove linamarin/cyanogenic glucosides). Compare to RSU-09 (Amazon Basin) for cassava as the shared carbohydrate of Amazonia.

staple_foods.name: paiche / arapaima fish

primary_metabolites.protein_content: 19.5-22.0 g/100g [Arapaima gigas — Núñez et al. 2016 Food Chem 211:595]

umami_compounds.glutamate: 0.08-0.15 g/100g free glutamate [freshwater fish — lower than marine]
umami_compounds.inosinate: 0.3-0.5 g/100g IMP [Arapaima — Núñez et al. 2016; high IMP in muscle]

staple_foods.notes: Paiche (arapaima) is one of the world's largest freshwater fish — up to 3m, 200kg — native to the Amazon basin. A culturally significant protein source for Amazonian Peruvian communities. High IMP content makes it an umami-rich fish despite freshwater environment. Now also farmed in Loreto region aquaculture.
data_sources: Núñez et al. (2016) — Nutritional composition of Arapaima gigas, Food Chem 211:595

staple_foods.name: masato (cassava beer)

organic_acids.lactic_acid: 0.3-0.7 g/100g [masato — LAB fermentation during cassava starch hydrolysis]

staple_foods.notes: Masato is the traditional cassava-based fermented beverage of Amazonian indigenous communities — produced by chewing cassava (salivary amylase begins starch hydrolysis), spitting into a vessel, and allowing wild yeast + LAB fermentation. The salivary amylase saccharification is a unique enzyme source not found in any other RSU fermentation system (vs koji mold amylase in rice wine/sake). Compare to chicha de jora (RSU-10, Andean corn, salivary amylase also used) and apong (RSU-21, rice, mold starter).

staple_foods.name: aguaje palm fruit

primary_metabolites.lipid_content: 12-22 g/100g [Mauritia flexuosa — Yuyama et al. 2003; high fat Amazonian palm]
primary_metabolites.ascorbic_acid: 35-60 mg/100g [aguaje — Yuyama et al. 2003 Food Chem 83:79]
primary_metabolites.oleic_acid: 70-80% of fatty acids [aguaje oil — very high oleic, similar to olive oil]

key_flavor_bioactives.tocopherol_content: 500-1200 mg/kg [Mauritia flexuosa — exceptionally high tocopherol; Zanatta et al. 2012 Food Sci Technol 32:505]
key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 150-300 mg beta-carotene equivalent/100g [aguaje — bright orange-red beta-carotene; Zanatta et al. 2012]

staple_foods.notes: Aguaje (Mauritia flexuosa, the 'tree of life') is the most commercially important wild plant food of the Peruvian Amazon — sold throughout Loreto and Ucayali as fresh pulp, ice cream, and flavoring. The tocopherol content (500-1200 mg/kg) rivals or exceeds argan oil (RSU-28, 500-900 mg/kg) — both are outlier vitamin E sources. The very high oleic acid content (70-80%) despite being an Amazonian tropical palm is unusual — most tropical palms are high in saturated fat (palm oil) or polyunsaturated. Compare to chontaduro (RSU-18) for a multi-Amazonian-palm fat comparison.
data_sources: Yuyama et al. (2003) — Chemical composition of Amazonian palm fruits, Food Chem 83:79
Zanatta et al. (2012) — Buriti (Mauritia flexuosa) pulp properties, Food Sci Technol 32:505

notes: Peruvian Amazon lowlands (Loreto / Ucayali region, ~120m). Alluvial parent material from Andean sediment deposited by Ucayali/Marañón rivers. Lowest altitude in the Peruvian altitude gradient (RSU-10 at 3500m, RSU-18 at 1600m, RSU-31 at 120m). Criollo cacao adds a fine-flavor variety vs Forastero comparison for the cacao natural experiment. Aguaje palm tocopherol completes the high-tocopherol fat trio (aguaje RSU-31 ~ argan oil RSU-28 > olive oil RSU-15). The salivary-amylase masato fermentation is a unique food technology shared with RSU-10 (chicha). Lowest industrial processing index in the pilot set (0.10) — closest to a traditional hunter-gatherer-agriculture continuum.
